title: Programming abstractions
html:
A cliché saying: “Everything is a trade-off”, and abstraction is no exception. The most common way to reach for one in OOP design is to abstract anything that can be named: “I’m gonna need to send a message, let’s introduce a message service; oh also a message factory; oh also a message dispatcher; oh how about …; on and on…“. Sometimes it works beautifully. Sometimes it quietly brings more downsides than it is worth.

Every abstraction is a bet

If you win, it is easy and faster to lay other changes on top of it.
If you lose, you now have a premature abstraction, and every change layered on top of it comes out smelly. Eventually, when changes pile on top of those changes, it starts to feel like a Jenga game where everyone already knows what the ultimate end looks like.

So, abstractions are not free. It feels good when you can name things and when you can adopt a newly-learned design pattern — that satisfaction is real, which is exactly what makes it dangerous. But remember it is like gambling, and more often than not, the odds lean toward the losing side. The more convoluted and complicated the software already is, the more your winning fraction shrinks.

The comprehensiveness cost

No matter how semantically and meaningfully an abstraction is named, it is probably harder to understand than the direct equivalent. The more intermediate layers someone must pass through, the more mentally and cognitively draining it becomes — especially for others who don’t share the same intimate level of familiarity with your implementation. And “others” here might just as well be you, a couple of months from now, staring at your own cleverness without the context that made it obvious.

This is what K.I.S.S. is about: keeping things stupidly direct, so that anyone can Ctrl/Command+Click and simulate the next step of how runtime execution would unfold, without having to hold a tower of indirection in their head.

Remember that you can always come back and refactor or abstract things later, if the feature ever actually evolves to need it. And when you do need to refactor, chances are you have a better understanding by then, having been through a few reality checks with the current code. It is always easier to refactor/generalize a specialized code than to do it in the reversed direction — untangling an over-engineered, over-abstracted design with other dependants already built on top of it.

Imagine a codebase as a living entity. It has its own lifecycle and its own way of evolving; it is not a product you deliver once and for all. Keep that in mind﹣that there is always a wrong abstraction risk and a comprehensive cost﹣when justifying decisions in your (or others’) design and implementation, and when doing code reviews. (A line of thinking sharpened for me in a friend’s garden of thoughts.)

Further Readings

https://sandimetz.com/blog/2016/1/20/the-wrong-abstraction

https://rachelbythebay.com/w/2021/09/05/clever/

https://www.csc.gov.sg/articles/how-to-build-good-software
